data.title: Intuit's $100B Panic: Premature AI Death Call
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Intuit Inc. (INTU) has lost nearly $100 billion in market value over the past six months, as the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative has taken hold of the market.

Investors fear AI disruption across software, rendering a lot of companies potentially worth a lot less as their main offering, software, becomes commoditized.

But I believe this framing is fundamentally flawed.

Intuit is not being disrupted by AI, but rather, it is embedding AI into one of the deepest proprietary financial datasets in the world.

Recent results do not support the narrative that AI is displacing Intuit, and after the sell-off, this stock trades at a very reasonable forward valuation.

I’m taking this chance to initiate coverage on Intuit and buy the dip.

The $100 Billion Panic

It’s often said that price creates a narrative.

With Intuit falling over 30% in the last year, we have to analyze the narrative that has been created around this.

Why pay for TurboTax if an AI agent can handle your return?

We can break down this AI argument further into three distinct forms of disruption.

First, there is the argument that the “seat-based” SaaS model is obsolete.

In a world of agentic AI, perhaps you no longer need multiple accountants logging into QuickBooks. One manager supervising intelligent agents could replace several paid seats.

Second, Intuit’s partnership with AI model providers could compress margins.

There’s an argument to be made that, when Intuit integrates AI into its products, it is essentially just selling a ChatGPT model trained for finance.

And lastly, in a world of rapidly evolving AI, a small business owner could potentially soon create their own AI bot that can handle basic bookkeeping and tax preparation.

Each of these concerns sounds reasonable in isolation, but the flaw lies in what they overlook about Intuit’s business model and true moat.

Intelligence Vs. Financial DNA

The market is confusing intelligence with context.

Large language models are good at general intelligence.

Yes, they understand tax codes and financial terminology. But they lack persistent, structured knowledge of a specific user’s financial life.

Intuit, by contrast, operates on proprietary ”Financial DNA” accumulated over decades.

Across QuickBooks, TurboTax, and its other products, the company holds tens of thousands of data points per consumer and hundreds of thousands per small business.

This data is what sets the company apart.

When a generic AI categorizes a complex expense, it makes a guess based on patterns it has seen in aggregate training data.

But when Intuit’s system processes the same transaction, it incorporates years of prior filings, industry benchmarks, payroll behavior, and compliance history.

In an AI-driven world, raw intelligence is cheap and easily replaceable. But proprietary context becomes more valuable.

It's All About Outcomes

AI is disrupting, and it’s increasing competition, which can squeeze margins, but it is also creating a lot more value.

As I have discussed before, the new era of software will be about providing an outcome rather than a service.

Intuit is successfully shifting from selling tools to delivering outcomes.

Instead of simply providing bookkeeping software, it is moving toward “done-for-you” workflows powered by AI agents.

For small businesses, time saved translates directly into productivity and cash flow improvement.

Moreover, it opens up the door to new markets.

AI and Generative AI, in particular, has accelerated our opportunities to serve those customers and move upmarket.

Source: Barclays Technology Conference

We can already see this happening with the Intuit Enterprise Suite, which is growing around 40% and enabling Intuit to compete against larger players like SAP (SAP).

On top of that, as the platform becomes more automated and embedded, switching costs may actually rise.

Replacing a basic ledger is easier than replacing an intelligent system that handles payroll, compliance, invoicing, and forecasting autonomously.

The Human Element Matters

And yes, the human element matters.

Firstly, because AI is not ready to be deployed across companies just like that. Most people don’t have the time or knowledge to simply replace all their current enterprise software with custom-built AI.

The point is to make life easier for businesses, not create more work for them.

Ultimately, when it comes to financial reporting, avoiding AI hallucinations is pretty important, and so is having someone who can be accountable for their mistakes.

Earnings And Valuation

Of course, AI will change software. It may make some companies more valuable and others less.

But after a 30% haircut, I definitely think Intuit is worth a stab here.

This is a company still bringing in revenues at a very healthy rate, with a large market they can still tap into and healthy margins.

And the valuation now seems very reasonable.

The PE is 18, and the PEG is under 1.0, with the forward PEG just above 1.0.

This is a business with durable margins, strong free cash flow generation, and, in my opinion, deep data moats.

The market is anticipating something that has not happened yet, and I think at least exaggerated the AI doom narrative.

Final Thoughts

All in all, AI will reshape software, but I view it as a positive more than a negative.

There’s merit to some of the bear arguments, but there are also reasons to be bullish, and at today’s price, the risk-reward favors the upside when it comes to Intuit.

data.title: Bristol-Myers: I'm Buying Post Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (NYSE: BMY) has been one of the great winners in the pharmaceutical company since I rated it a strong buy back in late October, as it successfully executed its strategic rewiring designed to navigate one of the most significant loss of exclusivity cycles in the industry. I’ll dive into why I believe that, despite the stock gaining nearly 40% since I last reviewed it, the company is still prone to going higher.

Current Dynamics

BMY released its earnings earlier in February and came out strong with a double beat, as Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.26, a beat of 6 cents. Revenue came out at $12.5B, a beat of $220MM, and a 1.6% Y/Y increase. More importantly, the growth portfolio has grown 17% Y/Y to reach $26.4B, now accounting for nearly 60% of total revenue. It has become clear that the trough of the company’s patent cliff is significantly shallower and more temporary than consensus estimates suggest. It is now large enough to absorb the generic erosion of assets like Revlimid and Sprycel, as it is currently demonstrating double-digit momentum.

Management has also demonstrated a strong ability to execute, as evidenced by the $1B in savings from the strategic productivity initiative in 2025, with another $1B targeted for realization through 2027. This discipline allows for debt reduction, as BMY completed a $10B paydown ahead of schedule. Plus, it has continued to find high-value business development, such as the acquisition of Orbital Therapeutics.

Diving deeper segment-wise, BMY continues to be a leader in oncology thanks to the durability of Opdivo and the emergence of next-gen combinations and modalities. Opdivo delivered $10B in annual sales for the year, supported by continued share growth in first-line non-small cell lung cancer. Furthermore, the success of Opdualag, which is a fixed-dose combination of nivolumab and relatlimab, is a critical component of the growth trajectory of BMY. It just achieved over $1B in annual sales and captures nearly 30% of the first-line metastatic melanoma market in the US.

On the Hematology side, the segment has drastically expanded through Reblozyl and Breyanzi, as well as the upcoming CELMoD agents. Reblozyl on its own generated $666MM in Q4, a 22% Y/Y increase thanks to its use in the first-line treatment of anemia in MDS patients. Breyanzi, which is a CAR-T cell therapy, also surpassed $1B in annual sales during the year, thanks to the FDA approval for follicular lymphoma and marginal zone lymphoma. I’m particularly enthusiastic about the CELMoD program, which will feature iberdomide and mezigdomide, where these oral protein degraders are designed to be more potent and better tolerated than existing IMiDs like Revlimid. The FDA has granted priority review for iberdomide with a PDUFA date of August 17. Success in this area could be a huge win for BMY, with the combination potentially reaching $3.6B by 2033.

On the cardiovascular front, the big crisis has been the looming patent cliff for Eliquis, which just generated $3.5B in Q4 for the year, though management's 2026 guidance for Eliquis growth of 10% to 15% demonstrates that the asset remains a potent cash cow in the near term. This actually surprised me, but this is further supported by the strategy to replace Eliquis revenue through Camzyos and the development of milvexian. In the case of milvexian, which is an oral factor Xla inhibitor being developed in partnership with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and intended to be the successor to Eliquis, it is set to achieve global sales of up to $6B by 2033.

Looking forward, the 2026 revenue guidance came in at between $46B and $47.5B, which notably exceeded the consensus of $44.2B. Though the legacy portfolio is projected to decline between 12% and 16% due to the removal of volume limits on generic Revlimid and the entry of generic Pomalyst.

Valuation

BMY’s valuation has always been part of my bullish thesis as the company generally was trading at the bottom of the industry metrics. Though the stock has increased by a vast margin over the past 4 months, BMY still has some of the lowest metrics in the industry, with a P/E Non-GAAP of 9.88, the second lowest in the group after Pfizer (PFE), and the same could be said for the company’s EV/EBITDA of 8.36. And though the company’s FCF yield is lower than in October, it still stands at an industry-leading 10.4%. The gap in valuation is still heavily linked to the patent cliff narrative, akin to Pfizer. Though compared to PFE, I believe that BMY is further in the cycle with an already strong performance in its growth portfolio that will outpace the losses linked to patent expiration. This lowered valuation also gives BMY a strong opportunity cost for patient shareholders with its current dividend yield of 4.13%.

The company successfully launched several therapies that achieved blockbuster status in 2025, with these brands currently growing at an incredible rate and being the primary engines offsetting the legacy generic erosion. For example, Camzyos grew nearly 60% in Q4 and is the first cardiac myosin inhibitor for oHCM and recently met its primary endpoint in a Phase 3 trial for adolescents, potentially expanding its market. The $14B acquisition of Karuna Therapeutics also positioned BMY as a leader in neuropsychiatry, with Cobenfy, the first muscarinic antipsychotic, being the centerpiece of this expansion. It surpassed 100,000 TRxs during the year, and the true growth potential lies in the expansion into Alzheimer’s disease psychosis, an area with 3.2MM diagnosed cases in G7 markets. Peak sales could eventually reach $8B annually in this case.

And though BMY is highly profitable like the bulk of its competitors, its gross margin is experiencing a temporary shift due to the transition from legacy to new growth. Gross margin is set to be slightly lower this year at around 69% to 70% due mainly to the loss of exclusivity for extremely high-margin products like Revlimid and Pomalyst. Though this is slightly offset by lower R&D and SG&A expenses, which dropped 8% and 1%, respectively. Though the company’s high Return on Equity is certainly attractive at +40%, thanks to the exceptionally efficient management.

Risks

There is still the patent cliff hovering over BMY’s head, with Revlimid already seeing sales plunge 55% in the final quarter of 2025. Eliquis, which is still growing, faces a US patent cliff in April 2028, plus the management is expecting a $1.5B to $2B revenue step-down in 2027. In all, LOEs could affect around 64% of BMY’s estimated 2025 revenue by the end of the decade.

On the regulatory side, the IRA has introduced government-mandated maximum fair prices for top-selling drugs, where already under pressure, drugs like Eliquis will see Medicare price negotiations begin in 2026. Plus, the agreement to provide Eliquis free to Medicaid in exchange for tariff relief materially alters the brand’s economics.

Conclusion

I believe that Bristol-Myers Squibb is a company that has navigated the eye of the storm, with the results of the year proving that the growth portfolio is not just a theoretical offset to legacy erosion but a tangible, high-performing reality that now dominates the company’s revenue mix. The ability to exceed the company’s conservative guidance while also reducing debt and increasing dividends demonstrates a level of operational excellence I’m willing to bet on. I keep my Strong Buy rating on Bristol-Myers Squibb.

data.title: Wyndham Hotels & Resorts Gets An Upgrade With A View, A Bright One
data.description:
One stock hitting my desk this week after its recent earnings results on Feb. 18th, when it beat estimates, is Wyndham Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (WH), which is also up around +6.8% since my bullish rating in January 2024.

What I loved about them then was stable dividend income and growth, the surge in travel demand forecasts in 2024, and revenue growth.

This time around, we'll be looking at more recent data and applying my 8-step methodology for a holistic follow-up on this stock. Because I often write about or invest in hotel REIT stocks, I think actual hotel brands like this and where they could be heading are also relevant to equity REITs in this sector, as well as a glimpse into the travel/lodging sector, which is a huge global economic sector.

Thesis Summary

My updated thesis this time actually reaffirms my prior Buy rating again and also agrees with the bullish consensus from both Wall St and SA analysts as of Thursday, despite the bearish view from the SA quant system.

Wyndham's key upside drivers could be a robust pipeline of room growth globally, a diversified brand portfolio across multiple spend categories, a strong EBITDA margin among peers that could drive earnings efficiency, as well as dividend safety/growth and positive upside forecasts to the share price. This bullish view comes despite the just-below investment grade rating from Fitch since last year.

The worksheet above summarizes my 8-step methodology, which reviews this stock over 8 categories to get a holistic score. Read on to learn more about each section.

Macro & Sector Outlook

First, let's consider some market impacts on the hotel sector.

Notable is a relatively confident outlook by JLL this month, who said the supply/demand dynamic could favor the hotel sector in 2026:

Slower supply growth across major markets will underpin performance of existing hotels, with most major U.S. cities showing construction pipelines below 2% of existing supply.

Further, while I've seen some comment that the 2026 World Cup could provide tailwind to hotels this year, which PwC also mentioned in an article, since that is a one-time tournament every 4 years I don't think it's the best sign of long-term sustainability but perhaps a near-term boost in the North America market.

The longer-term tailwind could actually come from AI use in this sector, which could have a direct impact on cost efficiency and thereby impact on earnings, not necessarily by eliminating the human element of the hospitality experience but by complementing it. For instance, PwC also wrote in the same study that:

On the operating cost front, AI is driving significant labor and operational efficiencies. Occupancy forecasting powered by AI allows hotels to better align staffing levels with anticipated demand, reducing overstaffing during slow periods and ensuring adequate coverage during peak periods.

Lastly, I would also consider the recessionary nature of this sector and the impact from consumer spending confidence, particularly higher-spending travel categories. This is relevant to Wyndham, which has a luxury and upscale component to their portfolio that includes the Registry Collection and Wyndham Grand.

My concern about that niche is that some survey data by Deloitte, published this month, showed that travel usage in the recent holiday season actually took a slight dip among the $100K+ income group, and this is actually a very busy traditional travel period of the year:

A mitigating factor for Wyndham, I think, is their highly diversified brand portfolio, which spans across different spending categories. Consider that Fidelity Investments wrote in their 2026 economic outlook that

"for all the doom-and-gloom about high prices that you might see in the headlines, American consumers aren’t letting it dampen their spending."

So, I am bullish in this section because despite the increased risk of lower upscale spend, the sector benefits from a supply/demand imbalance, could benefit from AI efficiencies, and has a resilient consumer.

Competitive Positioning

I picked the following 4 peers as good comparables for this stock: Marriott (MAR), Hilton (HLT), Choice Hotels (CHH), and Intercontinental Hotels Group (IHG).

The market cap range is wide, from $5B to $90B, with Marriott being the biggest and much bigger than Wyndham by market cap.

This niche has considerable focus on franchising their brands to others rather than actually owning all the hotel properties with their name on them, and Wyndham in their Feb. 2026 investor deck called this an "asset-light business model generating significant free cash flow."

Peer data does not put Wyndham in the best light, with a 5-year revenue CAGR at just under 6%, while Hilton and Marriott both exceeded 25%. Wyndham did somewhat better on return on equity (ROE) of around +58%.

In addition, I like to compare price return over the last decade vs a major benchmark like the S&P 500 (SP500) to get a sense of the potential momentum of a stock vs. peers, and what we can gather from the chart below is that Wyndham saw a positive price return of +32% in this period but severely underperformed the benchmark and all of the peers in this group, while Marriott and Hilton actually beat the benchmark:

Beyond these metrics, though, where I think Wyndham can compete in this niche is its continuing global growth beyond its home market of the U.S. For instance, its recent results press release said

"the Company's global system grew 4%, including 1% growth in the U.S. and 7% growth in the Company's higher RevPAR EMEA and Latin America regions."

Given the mixed picture above, I think it is worth being at least neutral in this section and not bearish.

Company Growth Factors

Next, let's talk about some more company-specific growth factors and impacts.

Even though it calls itself asset-light and its portfolio growth may not be tied to owning more property, like a hotel REIT may want to do, its growth can still benefit from new hotel developments that are planned to be future Wyndham-branded hotels, since it can drive future fees for the company.

In that regard, notable is the FY25 infographic showing a "record pipeline" across multiple categories:

Complementing this data is Seeking Alpha data showing expected forward revenue growth of +4.2%, beating the sector average and showing there is modest confidence about growth.

The Q4 results that came out this week also indicated "system-wide rooms grew 4% year-over-year," which is another key growth lever; however, new rooms and developments cannot ignore headwinds in another key hotel metric, RevPAR (revenue per available room), which saw a YoY decline. The company said, for example:

For the full-year, global RevPAR decreased 3% in constant currency compared to 2024, in line with the Company's outlook, reflecting a 4% decline in the U.S. and flat growth internationally. U.S. results reflected a 270 basis-point reduction in occupancy and a 120 basis-point decline in ADR.

So, the challenge for Wyndham for the rest of 2026 will be to show it can grow RevPAR again, and I believe a strong travel season this year could help. Notably, in their recent investor deck (pg. 18), the company has already forecasted improvement to RevPAR but also indicated some drivers of bookings this year:

So, Wyndham presents a growth case of potential recovery combined with pipeline growth, and for this reason I was bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

A key margin to compare with peers due to its impact on profitability and also giving us a sense of core business earnings efficiency is the EBITDA margin. If I had to choose one for this business, here is how Wyndham performed:

With an EBITDA margin of around +51%, Wyndham not only beat this peer group, but we also see the trend line has steadily risen since 2022, also indicating a nice recovery from the market shock to this sector due to the 2020/2021 pandemic era.

Unlike a hotel REIT, which might have property expenses as a key impact on earnings, this "asset-light" business of Wyndham does not even mention property expenses in its income statement, and we see from its balance sheet that property is not its biggest asset, but "goodwill" actually is.

Complementing this is consensus data showing estimates of between 6 and 15% YoY EPS growth this year and next, which I think shows confidence in this brand's ability to grow earnings.

We cannot, however, ignore the risk of one-time charges on this business when considering GAAP-based results, and it impacts headline metrics and shows in the recent results:

Full-year 2025 diluted EPS decreased 31% to $2.50 from $3.61 primarily reflecting non-cash impairment and other-related charges; however, adjusted diluted EPS increased 6% to $4.58, or approximately 6% on a comparable basis.

However, I am bullish in this section when putting aside those one-time accounting movements and focusing on the strong EBITDA margin, the adjusted EPS growth, positive analyst consensus, and the asset-light model.

Balance Sheet Risk

When it comes to balance sheet risk, being an asset-light business model does not mean without leverage, so here is a comparison of debt-to-equity vs. peers:

In this comparison, Wyndham's D/E of 4.50 is on the high end of this group, with only IHG considerably higher. However, this group also shows some companies with negative equity, like Hilton and Marriott, so there is a wide spectrum here, which means Wyndham is not exactly a major outlier.

Adding further to the risk lever is the fact that a major credit rating agency like Fitch has this company at just below investment grade at "BB+," which they reaffirmed in their Feb. update.

This comes after their May 2025 note, where they cautioned that "economic cycles and exogenous events can cause large declines in revenue and profitability for hotels."

So, I think a mitigating factor that could mute some of this risk is strong cash flow. Seeking Alpha data boosts confidence in that regard, showing expected fwd operating cash flow growth of nearly +13%, beating the sector average.

Further, from its investor deck (pg. 6), the company boasts the highest free cash flow yield among the peers shown, which is impressive:

Hence, I gave the stock a neutral view in this section because although they have a below-investment-grade rating and an elevated D/E above +4.0, they also have a strong cashflow story.

Dividend Outlook

Although not as high of a yield as some hotel REITs, Wyndham's roughly +2% dividend yield is almost in line with its sector average, so nothing remarkable.

Where it really impresses is not in yield but in growth, with a 5-year dividend CAGR of nearly +24%, easily beating the sector average, and also indicating to me a company with dividend resilience through the various cycles this sector goes through, considering that there exist peak travel seasons and slower periods.

In addition, with a dividend payout ratio of just 33%, this firm seems to have a lower risk of a dividend cut. Further supporting this is the positive EPS growth consensus for this year and next.

Hence, I am bullish in this section not for yield but for proven growth and dividend safety.

Valuation Metrics

Moving on, below is my worksheet using peer data from Seeking Alpha to compare valuation metrics but also to forecast a price target. Unlike a hotel REIT, whose important valuation metric is the P/FFO, in this case I'll be using the fwd P/E instead.

I am bullish in this section and calling this an undervaluation opportunity, and here is why.

I think the price/book comparison is less useful here due to a lack of adequate comparable data and this being an asset-light business, not one that holds a massive portfolio of owned hotels. So, the key metric is the fwd P/E, and Wyndham is undervalued compared to the peer average by 25%, even though I've shown earlier that it had the strongest EBITDA margin and also an analyst consensus pointing to potential EPS growth this year and next.

In addition, my price target implies a +7% upside, which is close to the +10% upside forecasted by the avg. Wall St rating consensus as of Thursday:

So, with undervaluation and both forecasts implying upside above 5%, I called it a Buy here.

Technical Charting

Moving past fundamental topics, let's see if the technical chart patterns will impact our thesis so far, which is overwhelmingly neutral-to-bullish.

In this chart, I circled a sell signal (death cross pattern) in red from last spring, and since then the 50-day and 200-day SMAs have been parallel; however, the 50-day seems to have pointed upward again as the share price spiked recently, likely in the wake of impressive earnings results.

In this case, I went with a Hold/neutral view because there is not a clear buy signal yet, just the early signs of strong momentum, but it could also retreat.

This neutral view lowers the overall score somewhat, but does not hurt the bullish case too much; it just makes it less aggressive.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

If I had to pick the one factor I think could have the biggest negative impact on my bullish thesis and directly challenge it, it would be an extended global recession impacting travel and lodging, which is essentially a discretionary spending category, and this can negatively impact room bookings and revenue.

A mitigating factor, I think, is that Wyndham has a global portfolio across multiple spend categories, and it is also likely that recessions do not have an equal impact globally but perhaps regionally or nationally. This is where I think the geographic and category diversification could help this company.

Conclusion

To wrap up, my updated review today reaffirmed why a bullish view should continue for this stock and what factors drive it, the most important ones being the robust room growth and development pipeline but also strong margins, in the context of being in a niche with some very big names like Hilton and Marriott, among others.

I view this stock as a dividend idea too this month, due to its proven dividend growth and safety, but it also remains an opportunity to own a piece of the hotel sector, while also understanding the unique risks of this sector.

data.title: SCHG: Recent Economic Data Shows Why This Is A Well-Positioned Fund
data.description:
This is a new economy. While traditional business cycles included broad-based economic growth across most sectors with people from nearly every income group doing well, the economy is much more bifurcated today. Higher-income households are doing disproportionately better than those in the middle and bottom, while the labor market is also seeing more concentrated strength in different sectors.

Investing was generally much easier over the last decade, with index options in most cases significantly outperforming financial alternatives. While certainly the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq should continue to be appealing investment choices for many, the best capital allocation strategies moving forward will likely be more focused on the best-performing industries, such as health care and technology.

One ETF that has consistently and significantly outperformed the indexes of recent years by targeting these sectors is the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

SCHG has offered investors total returns of 97.71% over the last three years, while the S&P 500 has offered investors total returns of 73.72% during this same time period. The Nasdaq has offered investors total returns of 94.78% since February 2023.

I last wrote about SCHG in September 2025, and I rated the fund a buy. Today, I am upgrading the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF to a strong buy. The buildout in AI should remain a core growth driver for some time, and recent economic data shows growth in the health care sector is very strong. The Dow Jones U.S. Large Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index that SCHG tracks should continue to outperform the index composites.

SCHG has an expense ratio of 0.04%, $50.04 billion in assets under management, and a yield of 0.38. The fund rebalances quarterly and reconstitutes annually. SCHG is invested 43.62% in technology, 16.57% in communication, 8.74% in health care, 7.38% in financials, 5.89% in industrials, 1.74% in consumer defensives, 1.34% in basic materials, 0.63% in energy, 0.41% in real estate, and 0.41% in utilities. Schwab's ETFs' largest holdings are NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META). These positions make up nearly 40% of the overall investment's assets.

The main reason why the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF has outperformed the broader indexes is the fund's fairly concentrated holdings in the large-cap tech sector. Still, while SCHG focuses on many of the biggest publicly traded tech companies, the ETF is still invested in different parts of the technology sector, with the fund holding corporations focused on the retail side with Amazon and Apple, as well as the software and chip side with NVIDIA and Microsoft.

The growth outlook for the tech sector remains strong, with AI expected to be a robust earnings driver moving forward for years. In the third quarter, large-cap tech companies reported historically strong earnings. The sector saw earnings rise nearly 25% year-over-year, while nearly 90% of corporations in the industry reported both earnings per share and revenue beats of consensus analyst expectations.

The tech industry is also projected to continue to experience strong earnings growth of 15-20% moving forward as well. While the earnings growth is expected to slow in the tech sector moving forward, the industry is still forecasted to both grow earnings at a double-digit rate and outpace the earnings growth of most other companies in the indexes.

The AI market is expected to grow at a compound annual rate of nearly 20% over the next decade, and large-cap tech should remain the strongest-performing sector in the market for some time.

SCHG also looks well-positioned outside of the tech sector. Recent consumer spending and economic data show the US economy remains stronger than most experts expected, and the evidence suggests President Trump's tariffs are having a very minimal impact on GDP. Economic forecasts for US growth are rising, with Goldman now expecting fourth-quarter GDP to rise at 2.5% on a year-to-year basis, compared to the consensus estimates for that time period of 2.1%. SCHG's more cyclical holdings in the financial and consumer cyclical industry look well-positioned.

Most of the recent economic and job growth has been in the technology and health care sectors, and these industries are expected to continue to be core growth drivers moving forward with the AI buildout and aging population. SCHG has nearly 52% of its assets in these sectors, and the investment's core positions in health care, such as Eli Lilly (LLY), have outperformed recently as well. The healthcare sector is being projected by analysts to grow at nearly 5% in 2026 and 2027 and 10% from 2027 to 2029.

All investment theses have risks, and SCHG is one of the more concentrated funds because of its heavily overweighted position in technology and the fund's significant position in large-cap tech holdings. SCHG is also a more cyclical ETF that invests in large-cap companies, making it more vulnerable to a possible trade war or a general economic downturn.

Investing was much easier for most of the last decade with broad-based growth across most major industries. Still, history tends to rhyme more than repeat, and with middle- and lower-income families struggling with higher prices and a more concentrated labor market, growth is likely to be more fragmented over the next several years. More targeted investing should produce better runs. Indeed, while SCHG takes a more targeted approach to capital allocation, this kind of strategy should appeal to most investors in this kind of more challenging economic environment.

data.title: Sunoco After Earnings: New All-Time Highs Within Reach (Upgrade)
data.description:
It has been years since I covered Sunoco LP on Seeking Alpha. I concluded an October 2, 2019, Seeking Alpha article with the following:

SUN has a profitable formula, which should sustain those juicy dividends for the foreseeable future. While the company is not in the oil patch with both feet, it operates on the margins of the sector. For those seeking dividends, the double-digit percentage yield at the current share price makes SUN an attractive stock, which could support its share price over the coming months and into 2020.

SUN shares traded at $31.32 in early October 2019, and the price has nearly doubled since then. In February 2026, SUN shares were over $61.30 and closing in on the March 2024 all-time high of $64.89 per share. I remain bullish on SUN as the stock continues to pay an above-market dividend, and its path of least resistance remains higher.

Shares Break Higher After Its Latest Earnings Report

Sunoco LP reported earnings on Feb. 17, and the market focused on EPS (normalized actual) that far exceeded forecasts.

The chart shows that EPS of $1.30 was well above the consensus forecast of $0.07 per share. Meanwhile, EPS (GAAP Actual) was $0.09 per share, missing consensus forecasts by $1.42. Revenues missed by $760 million at $8.60 billion. The earnings call stressed 2025 EBITA, excluding transaction-related expenses, which was at a record $2.12 billion, a 36% increase from the previous year. SUN also reported it has “begun the work to optimize our volumes in Canada and the Caribbean as we implement our gross profit optimization approach that we have evolved over the years.”

The company also reported improved 2025 performance at its new refining segment compared to previous years and that the refinery from the Parkland transaction is “an important piece of the supply chain supporting our market-leading fuel distribution business in Western Canada.”

The chart shows that SUN shares exploded higher after the latest earnings announcement on Feb. 17, rising from $55.07 to $62.42 on Feb. 18. SUN remained above $61 per share on Feb. 19.

An Inexpensive Stock That Pays a Healthy Dividend

According to Seeking Alpha, SUN has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 9.23 times earnings, which is below the sector median and far below the average P/E of the leading stock market indices, making SUN an inexpensive stock in an expensive market.

The company currently pays its shareholders a $3.73 annual dividend, translating into a well-above-market 6.06% yield.

Three Segments and Acquisitions Offer Growth Potential

Sunoco LP operates in three segments: Fuel Distribution, Pipeline Systems, and Terminals. While SUN exited the refinery business in 2012 to focus on fuel distribution and logistics, the company grew to become the leading fuel distributor in the Americas, transporting over 15 billion gallons of fuel annually to around 11,000 retail locations. SUN’s fuel distribution, pipeline systems, and storage terminals have positive growth prospects.

In 2024, SUN acquired NuStar Energy, expanding its midstream assets, which now include 14,000 miles of pipelines and over 160 storage terminals.

Meanwhile, SUN’s October 31, 2025, acquisition of Parkland Corporation for $9.1 billion integrated an expansive retail network and its Burnaby Refinery into SUN’s portfolio, allowing the company to dip a toe back into the refinery business. Sunoco exited oil refining in 2012 as crude costs rose and product demand slumped. However, over the past years, crack or oil refining spreads for gasoline and distillates have been elevated, leading to higher profits for oil refining companies.

Sunoco Stock is Approaching a Challenge of the All-time High

The long-term chart shows that SUN shares are in a bullish trend since the 2020 low and are approaching the all-time high.

The monthly chart highlights that the shares have moved within striking distance of the March 2024 record high of $64.89. At the $61.50 level, SUN had a market cap of $8.40 billion. SUN trades an average of over 419,000 shares per day.

U.S. Energy Policy Favors SUN and Other U.S. Oil Companies - Passing Seeking Alpha Factor Grades

U.S. energy policy under the Trump administration has shifted from inhibiting to supporting oil and gas production and consumption, favoring U.S. oil and gas companies and related businesses. SUN’s NuStar Energy and Parkland Corporation acquisitions are likely to be accretive over the coming months and years.

Seeking Alpha’s Factor Grades for SUN are attractive at an A- in the growth and a B+ in the valuation categories. While revisions earn a B and profitability a C, the momentum grade of a C- is too low, given the SUN’s recent price action and its bullish trend that threatens to challenge the 2024 record high. Given SUN’s over 6% yield, Seeking Alpha also assigns SUN a B+ grade in the dividend yield category.

It has been over six years since I wrote about SUN and recommended a "Hold." In February 2026, I am upgrading to a "Buy" recommendation as the shares offer value, the dividend is attractive, and recent acquisitions are likely to be accretive, adding to the company’s share price upside and earnings potential.

data.title: Home Depot Q4 Earnings Preview: Housing Trends In Focus, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Home improvement retailer, The Home Depot, Inc. (HD), will be releasing its Q4 results on Tuesday, February 24. Though shares in the stock have had a good start to 2026, up over 10% YTD, the stock has been relatively muted over the past month.

The flattish month is not necessarily a bad note, as the broader S&P 500 (SPY) is down 1% over this same period. However, nearby competitor Lowe’s (LOW) is outperforming, with gains of 15% and 1.3% YTD and over the month, respectively.

I last provided coverage on HD back in April 2022, and at that time, I was bullish on its prospects. I highlighted factors such as the age of existing homes and the long runway for renovations as one potential catalyst. The stock has since returned nearly 40% since that bullish update.

While I still believe the catalysts are intact, I am more neutral on the stock today heading into its Q4 earnings release.

HD Stock Key Metrics

When I last covered HD, shares were trading at around 20x forward earnings. In my coverage, I also stated that the price could approach $360/share.

HD today is sitting around and above that price point and at a forward multiple of 26x earnings. That is three turns above where LOW stands, and it is also 20% above HD’s five-year averages. I would argue that HD is fairly valued at today’s trading values.

Though both the broader Seeking Alpha (“SA”) analyst community and the associated quants would generally agree with my sentiment, those on Wall Street are more bullish.

The stock is still rated as an overall Buy, with those bullish seeing about 5% upside potential left in the stock. Upside is upside, but there are many, myself included, that wouldn’t get out of bed for less than 10%.

What Is The Outlook For HD Stock?

I believe that the overall tone heading into the Q4 release is optimistic but cautious. HD's stock performance over the past month reflects this.

Going back to Q3, comps at that time were essentially flat, with October turning negative as housing pressure and consumer uncertainty weighed on demand. Storm-related categories were also a drag, and that dynamic is expected to linger into Q4.

For the full fiscal year 2025, the management team had guided for roughly 3% total sales growth, slightly positive comps, a gross margin around 33.2%, and an adjusted EPS decline of about 5%. That’s not exactly inspiring growth, but it’s positive nonetheless.

Looking out to fiscal 2026, the company is guiding for 2.5% to 4.5% sales growth and flat to up 4% EPS growth, with comps flat to up 2%. Importantly, management layered in a recovery case tied to housing. If housing improves and larger project demand returns, sales growth could move into the 5% to 6% range with mid- to high-single-digit EPS growth.

At the same time, HD is tightening operations. Recent press releases included workforce reductions and a return-to-office mandate. This likely signals a focus on agility and cost discipline in the face of a challenged operating market.

What To Watch When HD Reports Q4 Results

When HD reports Q4, I’ll be tuning in closely to the comps. In Q3, August was solid. However, September cooled, and October turned negative. That deceleration is an important detail that investors should have noted.

Investors will want to see whether November and December stabilized, especially given management’s commentary around consumer uncertainty and ongoing housing pressure.

I’ll also be watching big-ticket trends. Transactions over $1,000 were up 2.3% in Q3, but the team noted softness in larger discretionary, finance-driven projects. If we start to see improvement there, that could signal pent-up demand beginning to release. On the flip side, continued weakness would reinforce the “higher for longer” housing narrative.

Margins are another key swing factor, in my view. Gross margin held at 33.4% in Q3, but operating margin compressed. With guidance calling for a full-year gross margin around 33.2%, any deviation, especially tied to promotional activity during Black Friday, will likely be scrutinized.

Finally, investors should listen closely to commentary around fiscal 2026. Management laid out base recovery and accelerated recovery cases tied to housing. Any shift in tone there could move the stock more than the actual Q4 print itself.

Is HD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At current levels heading into the Q4 printout, I view shares in HD as a Hold.

I still believe HD has a long-term tailwind tied to aging housing stock. I also like that the management team is continuing to invest through a tough operating cycle, expanding its Pro ecosystem, and positioning the business to capture share when the housing market eventually normalizes.

The issue, in my view, is valuation versus near-term growth. At roughly 26x forward earnings, shares are trading above historical averages and at a premium to Lowe’s, despite modest comp expectations and an anticipated EPS decline this fiscal year. Fiscal 2026 guidance calls for improvement, but even that base case assumes only low-single-digit sales growth and flat-to-modest EPS expansion.

Could housing reaccelerate and unlock the recovery scenario outlined in Q3? Absolutely. And if that plays out, the stock likely works higher. But today, I believe investors are already paying up for that possibility.

For those with a long-term horizon, I believe HD remains a core-quality name. However, given the current environment and valuation, I believe the risk-reward is just right. That keeps me neutral and comfortable rating the stock a Hold heading into earnings.

data.title: DoorDash: The Netflix Of Food Is A Sneaky AI Beneficiary
data.description:
DoorDash (DASH) has been one of the few pandemic winners to continue growing at a robust rate even following the pandemic. The company is solidly profitable on a GAAP basis and is investing in an integrated platform. I expect the company to end up being a clear beneficiary of AI. The stock looks quite attractive given the long-term growth runway. I reiterate my buy rating on the stock.

DASH Stock Price

I last covered DASH in November, where I upgraded it to a "Buy," calling it the “future of local commerce.” The stock has underperformed the broader market by around 9% since.

As the company enters a more mature phase in its growth trajectory, I expect investors to warm up to the company’s robust GAAP margins and likely expansion as it embraces AI.

DASH Stock Key Metrics

DASH is widely known as a food delivery platform, though it is rapidly expanding its reach to extend to groceries and all retail. In the most recent quarter, the company generated 39% YoY marketplace GOV growth to $29.7 billion, exceeding guidance of $29.5 billion. This represented a sharp acceleration from the 25% clip posted in the third quarter, but I note it mainly reflects the acquisition of Deliveroo. The company generated $780 million in adjusted EBITDA, coming in at the high end of guidance of between $710 million and $810 million. The company is undergoing a “margin reset” year in which it is not expected to show the margin expansion that investors have grown accustomed to in recent years, due to both the integration of the Deliveroo acquisition as well as management’s intentional investment to build one unified platform. That said, the company remains robustly profitable on a GAAP basis.

DASH ended the quarter with $5.5 billion of cash versus $2.7 billion of convertible notes, reflecting the acquisition of Deliveroo.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up to $31.8 billion in GOV, representing 37.8% YoY growth. Management expects margins to improve meaningfully in the second half of the year.

On the call, management outlined expectations for the “majority of the tech stack work completed this year.” Even accounting for the aggressive investments, management noted that (excluding Deliveroo) “2026 EBITDA margin is going to be up slightly compared to 2025.” The stock had initially sold off heavily after hours following the release but has rallied in the session, perhaps due to investors anticipating material margin gains over the long term. Management acknowledged the threat from Amazon (AMZN) Grocery, noting that “consumers prefer choice.” This is one area in which I differ from management, as I think price is likely to be the main driver over the long term, especially as AMZN brings its offering to be at least “good enough.” That said, management was very optimistic regarding their long-term growth runway, noting that they believe that they are just “a fraction or a single-digit percentage of what DashPass could actually achieve” in terms of the total eating and shopping occasions in a week.

Is DASH Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, DASH traded at around 30x this year’s earnings estimates.

That might not look cheap at all, but it is important to note that the company is sustaining aggressive top-line growth alongside long-term margin expansion.

During the pandemic, this was a high-flying tech stock without a clear path to profitability. Now, the company is robustly profitable, and AI may make it even more profitable. DASH invests heavily in both its platform infrastructure and customer service. To my naive eye, it looks like a perfect application of AI. DASH might not sell AI, but it looks poised to be a clear AI beneficiary, and thus in many ways, an “AI stock.”

As previously noted, I am growing less bullish on the company’s efforts to expand into grocery and retail in large part due to competition from AMZN. Even so, as a loyal DashPass customer myself, I see what DoorDash is doing to restaurants as similar to what Netflix (NFLX) has done to video media. I think it is inevitable for DASH and others to increase the market size of food delivery, as the convenience of eating out while you’re eating in comes at an increasingly affordable price. This is a highly symbiotic relationship between DASH and the restaurants, as it may decrease the amount of in-house cooking in favor of ordering delivered food. There is the potential that the business faces some pressures in the event of an AI shock to the job market, as some may still view food delivery services as a luxury. But I would view DASH as being a potential beneficiary from such AI job displacements, as it may be able to further increase its delivery network, allowing it to exert long-term pricing pressure as well.

I expect the company to achieve at least 20% GAAP net margins over the long term (roughly in line with consensus). I note that the company generated a 27% incremental net margin in 2025. I would expect margin expansion to be resilient long-term due to both operating leverage as well as future integration of AI. That places the stock at around 20x earnings. This looks like a conservative valuation relative to consensus estimates for around 19% growth beyond this year. I could see the stock re-rating to at least a 1.5x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’), implying around 50% potential upside over the next 12 months.

DASH Stock Risks

The company might be disrupted by autonomous vehicles. Perhaps restaurants (and customers) are willing to do some extra legwork to save on transaction fees. It is possible that DASH and Uber (UBER) enter a pricing war on both fronts. It is possible that AMZN eventually enters the food delivery market as well - the thought of large AMZN trucks delivering restaurant food to your doorstep does not seem so far-fetched.

DASH Stock Conclusion

While the threat of AMZN grocery may dampen the growth ambitions for the grocery and retail ventures, that might not be such a bad thing. These are lower-margin businesses relative to food delivery, and I see a big enough market there in its own right. The stock looks cheap due to the strong balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and robust growth profile. DASH is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: MDY: A Primer On The Oldest Mid-Cap ETF
data.description:
Introduction to the State Street SPDR S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust

The State Street SPDR S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust (MDY), which is backed by State Street Corporation, has amassed total assets under management of $25 billion since its inception in April 1995. MDY pays dividends four times in a year, annualizing to a little over 1% at the current price. It has an expense ratio of 0.23%

What Does MDY Do?

MDY, which has been around for over three decades now at the time of writing, is considered to be the oldest US mid-cap ETF in this industry, and it fulfills its objective by mirroring the performance of The S&P MidCap 400® Index [SM4I], which is managed by the index provider S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

Just like how the S&P 500 is supposed to represent the 500 largest companies within the US, SM4I is designed to represent 400 mid-sized companies that have cleared certain profitability (prospective stocks should have delivered positive reported earnings over the most recent quarter, as well as the aggregate of the four more recent quarters), liquidity (at least 250K worth of shares of the stock should have been exchanged in each of the most recent 6 months before evaluation, as well as an annual dollar value traded to float adjusted market-cap ratio of 0.75x and above, and market-cap (unadjusted market-capitalization range of $8-$22.7 billion, with a float adjusted market-cap that amounts to at least 50% of the unadjusted range) thresholds.

Investors should also note that the holdings of SM4I don’t overlap with that of the S&P 500 or the S&P SmallCap 600 Index, for that matter.

MDY’s goal of mirroring SM4I is fulfilled by its trustee (as part of the 0.23% expense ratio that MDY charges investors, note that 0.1% consists of fees that go to its trustee), the Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK), which purchases all the same 400 stocks from the index, weighing them in the same proportion (the trustee also makes changes to the portfolio as and when the index makes changes, even if it is on a monthly basis).

What Are the Standout Traits of MDY’s Portfolio?

Even though MDY's tracking index weighs its individual stocks on the basis of their free-float-adjusted market capitalization, we don’t see a great deal of top-heaviness, which is what one usually sees with the S&P 500. To elaborate, the top 10 of MDY only account for 7% of the portfolio; in contrast, the top 10 of the S&P 500 have a more pronounced weight of 38%.

The reason for this is because the differential between the largest and smallest stocks in the S&P 500 is quite wide (a differential of almost 800x in their respective market caps). Whereas in the case of SM4I, the differential between the largest and smallest stocks is only around 20x. Secondly, investors should also consider that if a stock from SM41 becomes too large or too small (by way of market cap), it automatically slips into either the S&P 500 or the S&P Small Cap 600, so constituents here are typically within quite a narrow bracket.

With regards to sector exposure, MDY is well spread out amongst 11 different sectors, but still, a little over one-fourth of the portfolio consists of industrial stocks alone.

From a market-cap breakup, even though the average market cap of this portfolio of around $10 billion is in line with the mid-cap threshold, note that a large proportion of the holdings (over 64%) consists of small caps.

Stylistically, the biggest tilt (43%) goes towards hybrid stocks (stocks with both growth and value characteristics such as strong sales and earnings growth, high payouts, low price-to-earnings multiples, etc.)

What Are the Risks Associated With MDY?

Since MDY was established way back in 1995, it was set up under the archaic structure of a unit investment trust (UIT), rather than an open-ended fund. The drawback of UITs is that they are legally prohibited from using any dividend cash they receive from its 400 holdings to buy more stock during a quarter. Rather, these dividends need to be parked in a non-interest-bearing account until the end of the quarter. This prompts something of a cash drag during bull markets and when MDY’s holdings are rising.

We’ll elaborate on this in a while (in the peer section of the article), but MDY’s expense ratio of 0.23% doesn’t make it a very cost-efficient option for those who want to play the US mid-cap equity wave.

MDY’s annualized volatility profile is around 430 bps more than the standard ETF, which suggests that the returns it typically generates over a year tend to vary a great deal and make it difficult for someone who’s resorting to long-term planning.

MDY also has a very unappealing profile; its dividend growth has lagged the pace of a median ETF across different durations, and the dividend it currently offers of just a little over 1% is not even half as good as what a standard ETF offers. Some of this is also a function of its target cohort, the midcaps, who are still reinvesting a large chunk of their excess cash back into the business (rather than sharing it with their shareholders).

Who Is MDY For?

Mid-caps are seen as a well-balanced class that captures the best of both the small-cap world as well as the large-cap world while also offsetting some of the risks associated with those latter terrains. For instance, mid-caps are generally further down in their overall lifecycle, with more mature business models, and don’t necessarily struggle with the capital-raising challenges that small caps typically face. They are also not as volatile as small caps. Unlike large caps that are also largely close to hitting their maturity stage, mid-caps also offer better growth potential and at cheaper valuations. In fact, the valuation-earnings trade-off that these mid-caps offer is better than what small caps or large caps offer.

Note that mid-caps of MDY offer the best long-term earnings growth profile (11.2%) out of the three cohorts, yet can be picked up at a valuation multiple that is 25% cheaper than large caps.

MDY would also appeal to those who like ETFs whose holdings are well spread out and not clustered around the largest names (as noted earlier, MDY’s top 10 stocks account for a very low stake of around 7%). This opens up a window to profit from the movement of a wide group of stocks, rather than relying on just a handful of them.

MDY would also appeal to investors who appreciate ETFs that genuinely track the indices they claim to track in their marketing material (most passive ETFs only seek to publicize this facet in their marketing literature but eventually fail to replicate the performance of their respective indices). MDY’s exemplary tracking is illustrated by the low tracking errors across different timeframes (one, three, or five years).

For a product that also claims to replicate its index, note that it doesn’t see considerable churn of its holdings, and this reflects the SM4I. Basically, note that while most ETFs incur annual turnover ratios of 30%, MDY’s annual churn is roughly half of it and suggests that it would appeal to those investors who like to see some stability in their long-term holdings (lower turnover also means lower transaction costs, which would otherwise leave an inimical mark on the returns).

MDY's Peers

MDY may be the oldest, but it certainly isn’t the only ETF that passively tracks the S&P MidCap 400 Index. We present two younger and cheaper alternatives, in the shape of iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) and State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 400 Mid Cap ETF (SPMD). SPMD is also issued by the same fund family—State Street—a decade later. What makes it different is its structure as the more flexible open-ended fund, which is how most ETFs are structured today. This enables it to avoid the cash drag that a UIT like MDY faces. It is also substantially cheaper than MDY at an expense ratio of just 0.03%. Though SPMD distributes on a quarterly basis like the other two, it has grown its dividends at a quicker pace and also offers a superior yield. SPMD also has the lowest degree of annual churn.

IJH, which is backed by BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), stands out for its AUM of over $111 billion and daily dollar volumes of nearly $1 billion a year and its very tight spreads. Relative to their respective AUMs, MDY sees the most daily volumes, which suggests that it is heavily pursued by those who like to make shorter-term investments.

Summary

MDY, the oldest ETF in the US mid-cap space, offers the best of both the small-cap and large-cap worlds, a tilt towards blended stocks, and a pretty compelling valuations-earnings tradeoff. However, MDY’s positioning as a trust is not ideal compared to other alternatives.

This article answers three main questions about MDY:

  1. What are the main features of MDY's portfolio?
  2. What type of investor is MDY suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  3. How does MDY compare to other products that passively track the S&P MidCap 400 Index?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Armada Hoffler's Portfolio Overhaul: What The 2026 Transition Means For Investors
data.description:
Introduction

As an analyst, I usually start my articles by going over the most recent quarterly results. This time I won't be going over the Q4 2025 results. Why? Effective March 2, Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH) will be rebranded to 'AH Realty Trust'. This will be a pretty meaningful repositioning of the REIT and we have to dive into the details to see whether this strategic evolution makes a lot of sense (or not). I will focus on the projected 2026 operational performance and implications of the decisions that have been made.

Let me explain what happened and what it means for investors.

From complexity to clarity: A more focused and predictable REIT

According to management, the renaming and rebranding of 'Armada Hoffler Properties' to 'AH Realty Trust' aligns with the company's evolution as a firm. In the latest Q4 2025 conference call, management said the following:

As you can see, management is planning to simplify its business model by divesting its multifamily portfolio and selling its construction management business as well as their real estate financing business. I already wrote about AHH's new management's focus on simplifying and deleveraging the company

Management is planning to use the sale-proceeds to pay down debt, redeploy capital towards retail assets and (perhaps) buy back shares.

There is a lot of demand for these assets/business segments that management is currently in the process of selling. It is important to understand that most of this is happening at cap rates around + 5%.

We are under an LOI for 11 of our 14 multifamily assets with a global real estate investment and management firm following a disciplined and targeted marketing process that began months ago, drawing strong interest from multiple credible parties...

… Despite the high quality of our assets, public market valuations do not reflect the underlying private market value of the portfolio. Exiting the multifamily portfolio unlocks significant embedded value by harvesting the arbitrage between public and private market valuations and accelerates our deleveraging program. In addition, the exit of our construction business is effectively complete, and we are substantially finalized on terms with the buyer.

Lastly, we have executed an LOI with an institutional buyer to acquire the interest in 2 of our 4 real estate financing investments, and we are in discussions with our partner to exit a third. The remaining investment is currently in the market with comparable cap rates in the low 5's.

Mr. market seems worried about AHH's sale of its low cap-rate and valuable multifamily business and I get it why this is the case. Office and Retail assets are perceived as less valuable and therefore trade at higher cap rates. But it is important to look at this from multiple angles and prevent yourself from exclusively focusing on the selling of great assets. The management of Healthpeak Properties (DOC) is doing the same thing right now. They are selling high-value 'outpatient medical' assets at good cap rates to buy into lab buildings in a post-COVID oversupplied market. Capitalizing on a (in their view) once-in-a-life-time opportunity. Or to quote DOC's management directly:

There's a big opportunity set there, and it's an awfully good time to invest in our view. But again, at the right price and the right submarket.

The point is this: do you want a REIT's management to sell assets when cap rates are high or when cap rates are low? If management knows their industry well and can anticipate on future industry trends (like the lack of office supply on which I will - again - touch at the end of this article), selling high and buying low makes a lot of sense. And this is what AHH's management is planning to do, among other things.

The most important question for dividend investors is: what will happen to the dividend, considering the fact that a lot of income-producing assets are about to get sold right now? AHH's management provides some guidance regarding this point, but this is the part that won't satisfy all investors.

Post transition FFO and dividend safety

Numbers can easily manipulate you, both in a positive and negative way. The same goes for AHH's 2026 FFO guidance. You can look at the pro forma 2026 FFO of $0.52 (at the midpoint) in the 2026 guidance presentation (page 2). The assumptions baked in this guidance are on page 3. When you use this FFO number as a valuation "anchor", you will understate the earnings power of the post-transformation portfolio. That is why I will go (and show) you the apples-to-apples comparison using the 2025 and post transformation 2026 FFO numbers.

As you can see, FFO will drop because the company is getting rid of the 'General Contracting & Real Estate Services' ($0.05). Selling this business segment reduces earnings volatility and helps the REIT focus on recurring and predictable rental income. The same logic applies to the real estate financing segment ($0.07). You are dealing with interest rate sensitivity, repayments timing, credit risk etc. It is a fine business (proven by the low cap rates), but it makes it a lot harder for Mr. Market to value Armada Hoffler accordingly. If the market doesn't award Armada with a higher P/FFO multiple because of its complex business structure, it makes sense to simplify it. REITs use share issuances accompanied with (preferably cheap) debt to fund future acquisitions and, because of this, grow FFO per share. Together with the sale of the multifamily portfolio ($0.13), Nareit FFO will drop by as much as $0.25. The mitigating factor will be coming from the interest savings and acquisitions (+ $0.11) that will mostly come from the big deleveraging that will happen once the sales are completed. You will arrive at a net decrease in 2026 Nareit FFO of $0.14 (or 18%) when compared to 2025.

Do you prefer volatile earnings to cover your dividend payments, or do you prefer a predictable (and slowly growing) dividend coverage? A superficial analysis will point out that AHH's management is not doing investors a favor, since FFO is dropping pretty dramatically! But you have to understand that the 2026 guidance does not tell you the entire story.

Let me give you a couple of examples to illustrate what I mean.

Deleveraging helps a REIT a lot. Not only does it attract more investors that are risk-averse and skip on highly leveraged REITs. It makes sense to prefer long-term (unsecured) debt at a fixed rate, rather than relying on complex derivative products.

And please don't forget that REITs are leveraged vehicles that need to refinance a portion of their debt every year (if the debt is laddered well). Deleveraging the entire company means that - going forward - there is the ability to refinance all future debt at relatively lower rates. The only situation in which I won't like a company selling great assets like AHH does is when it is being done in a bear market at fire sale prices. But just like the share issuance that was done back in September last year at $10.50 a share, management seems to be pulling the trigger at the right time, given the circumstances.

As you can see for yourself, the portfolio will become way more easy to analyze and value going forward. 95% of 2026 Net operating income (NOI) will be coming from a mix of A-tier office and Retail assets at premium locations. Mind you: the multifamily assets won't be leaving the premises, so the desirability of the locations won't vanish post-sale.

Let us dive into the dividend safety now. The dividend safety is certainly not going to be in the clear just yet. The AFFO pay-out ratio that includes recurring capital expenditures like property-related capex and non-cash net operating income adjustments (like straight-line rent), show you that we are at a payout ratio of 95%. You would like a REIT to be in the 60% - 85% range, depending on its lease structure and/or type of industry where it operates in. You demand a lower payout ratio for (mid-tier) office REITs and you can be fine with triple net lease structures. But that's another topic. Even though Armada Hoffler has A-tier assets (illustrated by the very high occupancy numbers reported this quarter again), a 50/50 blend of retail and office properties still warrants a more conservative payout ratio compared to net-REITs like W. P. Carey (WPC) or Realty Income (O). The payout ratio of 95% warrants a lower P/[A]FFO multiple in the coming years. But that's fine with me, since I don't invest in the short-term and neither should you.

The post-transformation AFFO includes a $0.05 per share non-cash stock-based compensation that is added back into the AFFO number. Excluding stock-based compensation, the dividend coverage - again - is extremely tight going forward. A potential (larger) share buyback can mitigate this impact, but we really have to see how this plays out.

Management provided a slide that shows the future [cash] NOI growth going forward. Management highlighted the potential for a $10 million annualized commercial NOI growth in the years ahead. An analyst on the CC asked the following:

... which implies around $150 million of acquisitions if you just assume 6.5% cap rate. So how do you plan to finance this?...

Management remained vague, implying that there will be debt capital available in addition to equity... but that would depend on the cost of equity...

The caveat is exactly that: the cost of equity. Time and time again, management said that they are not planning to reduce the dividend (again). Thankfully, they won't raise it either. The focus is on simplifying and deleveraging the company. But in the mean time, the dividend yield sits at 9%. Making it very expensive to make lucrative new investments. So the bullish NOI growth shown above only works when the share price climbs materially. I don't see this happening overnight. I believe that Mr. Market will have a "let's wait and see" approach regarding Armada for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

There are multiple ways to look at a REIT's valuation. I will use two multiples and a cap rate valuation to see where the intrinsic value per share lands for Armada.

P/[A]FFO multiple analysis

Back in August last year, I calculated the intrinsic value of AHH's common shares to be around $10.50 - $15.40, depending on your personal view of the company. Regarding the $15.40 price target, I wrote the following:

If you expect that Armada Hoffler will reach an investment-grade rating in a couple of years, all the combined efforts I summed up just now could theoretically lead to - let's say - a 14x P/FFO multiple. Since we are kind of guessing right now, this equals a 13% increase over the historic market valuation of this company. It seems reasonable to me in a bullish scenario.

I take the current 2025 FFO guidance at the high-point. Obviously the FFO will climb in the coming years, so we are still using a pretty depressed FFO to begin with. Even in this bullish scenario, I am trying not to be too extreme in my assumptions. But no matter how I look at it, you arrive at a fair value around $15.40 (14 x $1.10). A price target that I feel is very reasonable if you can hold on to your shares for at least 2 - 3 years.

I do think that my previous reasoning makes a lot of sense. But Armada Hoffler uses the NAREIT version of FFO in the 2026 guidance, that differentiates a lot from the normalized FFO that the company issues as well. During Q4 2025, AHH reported a 2025 Nareit FFO ($0.78) and its own 2025 Normalized FFO ($1.08). Ceteris Paribus, the 2026 Nareit FFO ($0.64) should equal a 2026 Normalized FFO of $0.89. Given the current (lowered) multiple of 12x that Fastgraph gives us (using the normalized FFO number), we arrive at a fair value of around $10.70. In line with my previous (conservative) price target of $10.50. Because we have to wait and see how the transition unfolds, going with the more conservative valuation approach is a prudent move.

But there is another way of looking at it. You can take the AFFO multiple that currently sits at 14.08x. When we take the 2026 AFFO guidance of $0.59, we arrive at a fair value of $8.30 per share.

Whether you take AFFO or FFO matters a lot regarding Armada Hoffler's valuation.

But it is important to understand the fact that we are using historic multiples and future [A]FFO numbers. The fact that the company's business model will be simplified, showing more predictable cashflow and a lower leverage, should warrant a higher multiple going forward. We are still talking about A-tier assets, illustrated by very high occupancy rates: Retail (94.9%) and office (96.4%). Even higher than the multifamily department (94.6%).

It is impossible right now to know where (and if) AHH's multiple will expand, but it makes sense to me if it does in the future.

Cap rate analysis

Regarding the 2026 guidance, management showed us the following (page 3):

  • $270 million in secured debt paydowns
  • $400 million in net unsecured debt paydowns
  • $50 million of Retail Properties with a cap rate range of 6.25% - 7%

When we take the midpoint of the 2026 NOI guidance, we arrive at $133.5 million.

We can assume that Armada Hoffler will make lucrative investments at attractive cap rates. Using the Q4 2025 numbers, the office segment showed a 150 bps higher occupancy rate compared to retail. The expected 2026 cash NOI growth rate also shows a faster growth. It makes sense to me to assume that the current cap rate of the blended retail/office portfolio should be around a 6% cap rate. Especially given the fact that new office supply is non-existent and AHH's assets are performing extremely well. When we use a 6% average cap rate on the entire post-transformation portfolio, the company value equals $2.23 billion.

The 2025 net debt including preferred equity came in at around $1.645 billion. When we subtract the debt paydowns of $670 million, we arrive at $975 million in net debt (including preferred equity). When we subtract the 2026 net debt from the total company value ($2.23 billion), we arrive at an equity value of $1.255 billion. The current market cap sits around $640 million (at $6.16 a share - at close on Feb. 18). To be conservative, I will take a 15% discount to the equity value/net asset value per share, we arrive at a value of $10.20 per share. The conservative value per share should be around $8.30 - $10.70. Shares could expand by around 35% - 74%. In the meantime, you get paid a 9% dividend.

Keep in mind that it makes sense for Armada to trade at a higher future P/[A]FFO multiple because of the lower leverage, predictable cashflow and high quality portfolio. During the conference call, it became clear as well that many leases have below-market rent structures. Meaning that future rent growth can become pretty meaningful. It also means that there is a very low probability that future rents will decrease, like we've seen with REITs like Medical Properties Trust (MPT) or Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN).

Conclusion

It is hard to know how the market will ultimately respond to Armada's company transition. I am a firm believer that shares will trade materially higher in the coming years. But I do have to say that the dividend coverage is tight. Some investors might not like the fact that the multifamily part of the portfolio is getting sold and future cash NOI growth depends on the cost of equity, which is high at the moment and will hammer per share FFO growth going forward.

AHH will be a smaller, more conservatively led, and leveraged REIT going forward. It is hard to tell how this will resonate with the investor base in the near future.

For those investors that have a long-term investment horizon, just look at the graph below and tell me what will happen in the years ahead. Will re-leasing spreads on AHH's A-tier retail and office properties be high or low? And will cap rates expand or fall because of the upcoming supply/demand mismatch? I think that most of us know the answer. All it takes right now is patience.

Successful investing takes time, discipline and patience. No matter how great the talent or effort, some things just take time: You can't produce a baby in one month by getting nine women pregnant.

As always, thanks for reading.

data.title: Angie Holdings Limited Targets IPO On Faltering Revenue Growth, Thin Capitalization
data.description:
Angie Holdings’ Financial Results Are Worsening

Angie Holdings Limited (AGIE) has filed proposed terms for a $17.7 million US IPO, according to an amended F-1/A registration statement.

The company sells corporate uniforms to customers located in Hong Kong, China.

AGIE’s revenue growth has stalled, it has thin capitalization, its net results are deteriorating, and management is seeking excessive valuation multiples at IPO.

My outlook on the AGIE IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Angie Holdings Do?

The firm designs and sells a wide variety of uniforms for schools, businesses, and other commercial applications in Hong Kong.

Angie was founded in 2015 and is led by Chairman and CEO Mr. Wai Sing FONG, who has developed a collaboration with PolyU to create improved heat-resistant clothing, which is especially relevant for the construction and related industries.

The firm’s offerings include:

  • Corporate uniforms

  • Design services

  • Tailoring services

  • Functionality counseling

The company's revenue contribution by product type and location is shown in the pie charts below:

However, the company's customer base is significantly concentrated, with only five customers accounting for 31% of total revenue for the first half of 2025.

Also, Angie does not typically have long-term contracts with its customer base, so customers have lower switching costs, presenting a higher risk of loss to the firm as a result.

What Is Angie’s Market?

The global workwear market was an estimated $19.2 billion in 2025 and is forecasted to exceed $28 billion by 2033.

If accomplished, the CAGR of the worldwide market would be 4.9% from 2026 to 2033.

The Hong Kong market would likely be well under 1% of the total global market, although the larger Asia Pacific region accounted for the largest share of the global market, at around 44.7% in 2025.

By application type, the construction workwear market, which is the focus for Angie, accounted for 23.75% in 2025.

The growth of the market is expected due to increasing industrialization, continued urban infrastructure development, and growing demand for branded and customizable workwear solutions.

The historical and projected future growth path of the worldwide workwear market is shown in the chart below by type of product:

The company faces over 200 competitors in the Hong Kong market alone, including integrated providers and industry-specific vendors.

Competitor companies include:

  • The Group

  • iGift

  • K&S Uniform Company

  • Antonhill Company

  • Easy-Order

  • 101 Uniform Company

  • Workwear

  • MCSportsWear & Uniform

  • Beverly Apparel

  • Others

The company's strengths include its asset-light business model, strategic R&D partnership with Hong Kong Polytechnic, and customization expertise.

Its weaknesses are customer and geographic concentration, dependency on third-party manufacturers, and small size.

Management believes its opportunities lie in the potential for overseas expansion, a growing overall demand for functional apparel, and integrating technologies into its supply chain and products.

Threats include intense competition from hundreds of competitors in the Hong Kong market, regulatory and political risks regarding PRC implementation of laws in Hong Kong, and supply chain disruptions.

Angie’s Recent Financial Results

The company's topline revenue growth results have been disappointing, with either an outright contraction or slight growth in recent periods.

Operating and net profit have also dropped, and cash flow from operations has been uneven, as the table shows here:

Angie’s IPO Plan

The company intends to sell 2.73 million shares of common stock at a proposed midpoint price of $6.50 per share, for total gross proceeds of approximately $17.7 million, excluding underwriter allotments.

No existing shareholders or potentially new large shareholders have indicated an interest in the IPO, at least as disclosed in the IPO filing.

Company, director, and shareholder (greater than 5%) lockups will be for 180 days after the closing of the offering.

Management intends to use the net proceeds of the IPO as follows:

Approximately 25% for R&D of new functional materials;

Approximately 25% for R&D in using AI and/or software or mobile application to enhance automation of operation;

Approximately 20% for branding and marketing;

Approximately 15% for setting up representative offices in foreign countries such as France, Singapore and Dubai; and

The remaining 15% to fund working capital and for other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

The sole listed underwriter of the IPO is Cathay Securities, a firm that frequently seeks to bring small Asian companies public in the US.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of June 30, 2025, AGIE had $27,482 in cash and $1.9 million in total liabilities.

For the trailing twelve-month period ending June 30, 2025, AGIE generated free cash flow of just over $1 million on zero capex spend.

But with management’s focus on R&D spending with the proceeds, that will likely change or result in greater opex spending.

Prior to the IPO, the company's liabilities have been growing, while cash trends have been downward, suggesting a motivation for the IPO being to recapitalize the firm.

So, although the company generated over $1 million in free cash flow in the trailing twelve months, it has likely devoted the bulk of that to inventory growth and credit extension to customers, among other uses.

Angie’s Growth Is Stalled And Profits Are Dropping

Angie’s financial results are deteriorating just as it is seeking US public capital market investment, a negative signal for prospective investors.

The company's topline revenue growth has evaporated or turned negative in recent years, while operating and net results have worsened in the most recent half-year period.

Management seeks to grow through developing new functional materials via its increased R&D spend, expanding its overseas presence, and offering related services.

AGIE is exposed to all manner of risks, from customer concentration, geographic concentration, unpredictable Chinese regulatory actions, intense competition, and expansion risks.

It operates in a market that is expected to grow only moderately, and management plans to use most of the IPO’s proceeds for R&D expenses, which may not bear fruit for some time.

The company is also a ‘foreign private issuer’ and an ‘emerging growth company.’ These designations allow management to produce less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly immediately after their IPO, and Asian firms in particular have shown a habit of communicating very little with investors.

Also, a number of these firms have subsequently delisted their stock not long after their IPO, further destroying shareholder value.

Management is asking IPO investors to pay an EV/EBITDA multiple of nearly 48x on falling operating and net profits, surely an excessive ask.

With faltering revenue growth, worsening operating and net results, thin capitalization, and excessive valuation expectations, my outlook on the AGIE IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced
